content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 2, 1992

Maurice E. Huguet, Jr.
Turner, Huguet and Brans
924 Main Street
P. O. Box 110
Martinez, CA 94553

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-555

Dear Mr. Huguet:

This is in response to your letter requesting confirmation of
telephone advice provided to you on behalf of Clayton City
Engineer Rick Angrisani concerning his duties under the conflict-
of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act").1
Since you have not requested advice with respect to a specific
decision, we are treating your letter as a request for informal
assistance.2

QUESTION

Will Clayton City Engineer Angrisani have a conflict of
interest with respect to decisions affecting a person from whom he
leases property?

CONCLUSION

Mr. Angrisani will have a conflict of interest with respect
to any decision which will have a foreseeable and material
financial effect on his leasehold interest. Mr. Angrisani will
not have a conflict of interest with respect to his landlord so

________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-91-555
Page 2


long as the landlord is not a source of income or gifts of $250 or
more to Mr. Angrisani.

FACTS

Rick Angrisani is principal owner of Angrisani, Inc. and also
serves as the Clayton City Engineer by contract. Mr. Angrisani is
considering leasing an unimproved 6,000 square foot lot for a
three-year period from a major land developer in the city. Mr. Angrisani plans to locate a mobile office on the lot to serve
as his city engineer office. You stated that Mr. Angrisani would
pay rent for the lot at the fair market rate to the developer.

Mr. Angrisani has become concerned that the arrangement may
result in a conflict of interest should the developer appear
before the city on a matter that Mr. Angrisani would participate
in as city engineer.

DISCUSSION

This letter confirms that your letter dated December 11,
1991, accurately summarizes the telephone advice I provided to you
on December 10, 1991. As we discussed, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official at any level of state or local
government from making, participating in making or in any way
attempting to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on an economic interest.

Section 87103 provides that Mr. Angrisani has a financial
interest in any decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on:

(1) Mr. Angrisani or a member of his or her immediate
family;

(2) Any business entity in which Mr. Angrisani has an
investment worth $1,000 or more or holds a position as a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or manager (Sections 87103(a)
and (d));

(3) Any real property in which Mr. Angrisani has an interest
worth $1,000 or more (Section 87103(b)); or,

(4) Any source of income or gifts of $250 or more to
Mr. Angrisani within 12 months prior to the time when the decision
is made. (Sections 87103(c) and (e).) The definition of "income"
includes loans and forgiveness of indebtedness. (Section 82030.)
Moreover, "income" includes a pro-rata share of any income of any
business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns,
directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or

Our File No. I-91-555
Page 3




greater.³ Section 82028 defines "gift" to include a rebate or
discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or
discount is made in the regular course of business to members of
the public without regard to official status.

As I advised, Mr. Angrisani will have a real property
interest in the lot in question which is potentially
disqualifying.⁴ (Section 82033; Section 87103(b); Regulation
18233.) Thus, he may not participate in any decision which will
have a foreseeable and material financial effect on his leasehold
interest. (See, Regulation 18702.1; Regulation 18702.4.)

Moreover, if the developer is a source of income or gifts of
$250 or more to Mr. Angrisani within 12 months prior to the time
when a decision is made, the developer is a potentially
disqualifying economic interest. (Sections 87103(c) and (e).)

Absent an economic interest in the developer, or a
foreseeable material financial effect on some other economic
interest of Mr. Angrisani (such as his leasehold), Mr. Angrisani
is not precluded from participating in a governmental decision by
the conflict of interest provisions of the Act.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to call me at (916) 322-5901.⁵

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa

_______________
³ Thus, if Mr. Angrisani owns 10 percent or more of Angrisani,
Inc., and a person has been a source of income to the business, a
pro-rata share of the income is treated as income directly to
Mr. Angrisani. If Mr. Angrisani's pro-rata share is worth more
than $250 over the past 12 months, the person is a source of
income to Mr. Angrisani. You have not asked about clients of
Angrisani, Inc. If issues do arise, please contact us for follow-
up advice.

⁴ Regulation 18729 provides that the value of a leasehold
interest is the amount of rent owed during a 12-month period.

⁵ Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.analysis:
This letter confirms that your letter dated December 11, 1991, accurately summarizes the telephone advice I provided to you on December 10, 1991. As we discussed, Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official at any level of state or local government from making, participating in making or in any way attempting to use his official position to influence a governmental decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on an economic interest.

Section 87103 provides that Mr. Angrisani has a financial interest in any decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on:

(1) Mr. Angrisani or a member of his or her immediate family;

(2) Any business entity in which Mr. Angrisani has an investment worth $1,000 or more or holds a position as a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or manager (Sections 87103(a) and (d));

(3) Any real property in which Mr. Angrisani has an interest worth $1,000 or more (Section 87103(b)); or,

(4) Any source of income or gifts of $250 or more to Mr. Angrisani within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Sections 87103(c) and (e).) The definition of "income" includes loans and forgiveness of indebtedness. (Section 82030.) Moreover, "income" includes a pro-rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or

greater.3 Section 82028 defines "gift" to include a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official status.

As I advised, Mr. Angrisani will have a real property interest in the lot in question which is potentially disqualifying.4 (Section 82033; Section 87103(b); Regulation 18233.) Thus, he may not participate in any decision which will have a foreseeable and material financial effect on his leasehold interest. (See, Regulation 18702.1; Regulation 18702.4.)

Moreover, if the developer is a source of income or gifts of $250 or more to Mr. Angrisani within 12 months prior to the time when a decision is made, the developer is a potentially disqualifying economic interest. (Sections 87103(c) and (e).)

Absent an economic interest in the developer, or a foreseeable material financial effect on some other economic interest of Mr. Angrisani (such as his leasehold), Mr. Angrisani is not precluded from participating in a governmental decision by the conflict of interest provisions of the Act.
